title: Judgment, But No Repentance
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 04-27-25 -PM- Judgment, But No Repentance - Hosea 5-8-15 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. The Warning of War (vv. 8-9)
subpoints: Ram's horn and trumpet sound from Gibeah to Ramah to Beth Aven: an enemy approaches from the south
Despite the alarm, Israel's destiny is certain — God says 'I make known what is sure' (v. 9)
God says something once and means it; his promises and his curses are equally certain
The desolation of Ephraim will come at the hands of the Assyrians (2 Kings 17:5-6)

outline.title: 2. Judah's Sin (v. 10)
subpoints: The princes of Judah are like those who remove a landmark — God will pour out his wrath like water
Historical context: between 733 and 701 BC, Judah opportunistically takes land from weakened Israel
God had assigned each tribe its territory; to move the boundary is to unsettle what God settled (Deuteronomy 19)
Application: discontent with what God has given leads us to move boundaries he has placed for our good

outline.title: 3. Israel's Sickness (vv. 11-13)
subpoints: Ephraim is oppressed and broken because he only walks by human precept — or 'determined to go after filth' (ESV)
God will be to Ephraim like a moth and to Judah like rottenness — he will not bring a plague, he will be the plague
Seeing their wound, Israel turns not to God but to Assyria and King Jareb (the Great King) — who cannot cure them
Application: how often do we turn to something other than God and his Word when things go wrong?

outline.title: 4. The People's False Hope (v. 13)
subpoints: Israel knows the suffering comes from God, yet turns to the very nation that will destroy them for help
The greatest king on earth cannot help when God himself is the source of affliction
We too turn to food, substances, people, sex, work — anything but God — when life is difficult
Isaiah 45:22: 'Look to me and be saved, all you ends of the earth, for I am God; there is no other'

outline.title: 5. God's Judgment and Its Redemptive Goal (vv. 14-15)
subpoints: 'I, even I, will tear them and go away' — the double pronoun emphasizes that this is God's direct doing, not merely the enemy's
The Northern Kingdom was never restored from Assyrian captivity — a total historical judgment
God removes himself in verse 15: 'I will return to my place till they acknowledge their offense'
Yet even this withdrawal has a redemptive goal: 'then they will seek my face' and 'earnestly seek me'
Seek the Lord while he may be found (Isaiah 55); Christ promises rest to all who come to him (Matthew 11:28-30)

summary: An afternoon sermon on Hosea 5:8-15, in which the imagery shifts from the courtroom (vv. 1-7) to the field of battle. Five things are observed. First, the warning of war (vv. 8-9): trumpets sound from the south; God declares Israel's desolation certain — what God says once is enough (fulfilled historically by Assyria, 2 Kings 17). Second, Judah's sin (v. 10): the princes of Judah move boundary markers, opportunistically annexing Israel's territory during the Assyrian crisis; this reflects discontent with what God has allotted. Third, Israel's sickness (vv. 11-13): Ephraim's self-willed walk makes God himself the plague upon them — not merely an instrument of plague; the wound is putrefying, pre-antibiotic and lethal. Fourth, the people's false hope (v. 13): knowing the affliction comes from God, Israel turns not to God but to Assyria and King Jareb — who cannot heal them; we do the same when we turn to anything other than God for relief. Fifth, God's judgment and its redemptive goal (vv. 14-15): God tears and withdraws, emphasizing his own direct agency; the Northern Kingdom was never historically restored. Yet even this withdrawal aims at repentance: 'till they acknowledge their offense, then they will seek my face.' The sermon closes with the invitation of Matthew 11:28-30.
transcript:
## The Shift from Courtroom to Battlefield

If you paid attention to the reading this morning and then the reading this afternoon, you see that there is a change in tone and grammar in Hosea's prophecy. That change happens in verse 8, where we switch from the court of law (vv. 1-7) to the field of battle (vv. 8-15). Five things to see this afternoon: the war's warning, Judah's sin, Israel's sickness, the people's false hope, and God's judgment.

## 1. The Warning of War (vv. 8-9)

'Blow the ram's horn in Gibeah, the trumpet in Ramah! Cry aloud at Beth Aven, look behind you, O Benjamin!'

Two instruments — the ram's horn and the brass trumpet — sound the alarm. An enemy seems to be approaching from the south, since Gibeah, Ramah, Beth Aven, and Benjamin are all at the southern border of the Northern Kingdom. These verses may refer to Judah's incursion into Israel during the Assyrian crisis.

But regardless of how much they blow the trumpet, Israel's destiny is certain: verse 9 says 'I make known what is sure.' God does not need to repeat himself. He says something once and means it. His promises are yes and amen — and so are his curses. The desolation of Ephraim will come, and it will come at the hands of the Assyrians. 2 Kings 17:5-6 describes it: the king of Assyria took Samaria and carried Israel away, placing them in Halah and Habor on the river of Gozan and in the cities of the Medes. That captivity involved hooks through the prisoners' noses — a chain of humiliated captives. This is no pleasure trip.

## 2. Judah's Sin (v. 10)

'The princes of Judah are like those who remove a landmark; I will pour out my wrath on them like water.'

Sometime between 733 and 701 BC — while Assyria was dismembering the Northern Kingdom — Judah opportunistically moved into Israel's territory and seized land. God had assigned each tribe its territory. To move a boundary marker is to unsettle what God had settled. Deuteronomy 19 puts it directly: 'You shall not remove your neighbor's landmark which the men of old have set.' Moving into another's territory was stealing from a neighbor, and it showed discontent with what God had provided.

We do this too. We are never content with the situation God has placed us in. We start dating unbelievers because we cannot wait on God for a spouse. We work on the Lord's Day for unnecessary income because we do not trust God to provide. That is moving boundaries. That is not being content with what the Lord has given.

## 3. Israel's Sickness (vv. 11-13)

'Ephraim is oppressed and broken in judgment, because he willingly walked by human precept.' The ESV reads: 'because he was determined to go after filth.' Either way, Ephraim willingly followed wicked human precepts instead of being faithful to the Lord.

As a consequence, God says he will be to Ephraim like a moth and to Judah like rottenness. Notice: he does not say he is going to send a plague. He says he will be the plague. Instead of being a blessing, God's very presence becomes a plague — like a putrefying wound, pre-antibiotics, eating the flesh, oozing pus. That is what it means to have God as your worst enemy when he was once your husband.

And yet, even in the middle of all of this, when the people of Israel realize they have been judged by God, they do not turn to him. They turn instead to Assyria and send to King Jareb — 'the great king' — for help (v. 13). As if the greatest king on earth could cure a wound that God himself has inflicted. They know this is from God, yet they turn to the nation that will eventually destroy them. This is actually recorded in 2 Kings 15, where Israel sent money to Assyria hoping God's judgment would be removed.

And how often do we do the same? We turn to food, to substances, to people, to sex, to work — all the while God says, 'Look to me and be saved, all you ends of the earth, for I am God; there is no other' (Isaiah 45:22).

## 4. The People's False Hope (v. 13)

Israel's hope is in Assyria — when it should be in the Lord. The greatest power on earth cannot help when God himself is the source of affliction. And this is a pattern we see again and again in our own lives: we know things are going wrong because of sin, we know we need God, and we turn to anything and everything else.

## 5. God's Judgment and Its Redemptive Goal (vv. 14-15)

'For I will be like a lion to Ephraim, and like a young lion to the house of Judah. I, even I, will tear them and go away; I will take them away, and no one shall rescue. I will return again to my place till they acknowledge their offense; then they will seek my face; in their affliction they will earnestly seek me.'

Notice the double pronoun: 'I, even I.' The emphasis is deliberate. This is not ultimately Satan's doing or the enemy's doing. God himself will tear them. In history, the Northern Kingdom was never restored. They never came back from Assyrian captivity. And no one could do anything about it — 'no one shall rescue.'

Apart from Christ, this is the condition of every human being. God has that attitude toward those who are apart from Christ. But praise God that for those who are in Christ, the Father turned his face away from the Son on that faithful Friday — 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — so that he could look on us in love.

And even this withdrawal is not the last word. God says: 'I will return to my place till they acknowledge their offense.' This is not a permanent departure. Throughout the book of Hosea, judgment has a redemptive goal — God withdraws to draw them back. 'Then they will seek my face; in their affliction they will earnestly seek me.' This is the same promise God gives in Isaiah 55: seek the Lord while he may be found, call upon him while he is near. And the Lord Jesus makes the same promise in Matthew 11:28-30: 'Come to me, all you who labor and are heavy-laden, and I will give you rest.'

## Summary and Applications

Hosea 5 tells us that God is about to judge Israel through the Assyrians because of his people's prideful idolatry. Yet this judgment has a redemptive aspect — some will return to the Lord. All of it points forward to the Messiah.

From this morning: examine where you have been setting traps for others, look at the pride in your own heart, stop trusting in outward ordinances, raise your children in the faith.

From this afternoon: two more questions. Are you content in the Lord? Or are you moving the markers he has established in your life? And are you turning to something other than God as your hope — your own 'Assyria' — rather than to the Lord Jesus Christ?
